avoid: Do not attempt a custom full d x d GEMM for W @ X; that is high risk and unlikely to beat cuBLAS here.
Do not promote a universally scalar kernel as the default optimization path.
Do not assume smaller block size automatically reduces overhead enough to matter end-to-end.
Do not hardcode specific hidden dimensions or tensor values.
evidence: Correctness is perfect on all tested shapes, so the candidate is functionally valid.
Aggregate speedup is 0.9876, meaning it is consistently slower than the torch baseline rather than showing noise-limited parity.
All three tested sizes regress similarly: 3584 speedup 0.9886, 4096 speedup 0.9878, 4608 speedup 0.9863.
Uniform regression across shapes suggests the issue is systemic overhead or lower efficiency, not a single bad shape.
This family keeps cuBLAS for W @ X, so the remaining opportunity is the rank-16 path and elimination of the extra add pass.
Because total runtime is still dominated by the large d x d GEMM, any custom fused update must be extremely efficient to win; this scalar version does not recover enough time.
Vector width is 1 and the strategy explicitly chose an alignment-safe scalar kernel, which likely sacrifices memory throughput versus vectorized loads/stores for the d x d output update.
The smaller block size 128 did not translate into lower end-to-end latency, indicating launch geometry reduction alone is insufficient.
The slight worsening at larger d is consistent with memory-bandwidth-limited scalar output writes becoming more visible as tensor size grows.
recommended_actions: Do not promote this candidate as a default path; keep it only as a correctness-safe reference if needed.
Focus optimization effort on the rank-16 update path only, not on replacing the full W @ X GEMM.
Add shape-aware dispatch so vectorized kernels are used when alignment permits, especially float4 loads/stores for d divisible by 4, while retaining a scalar fallback for safety.
Revisit launch geometry jointly with vectorization and memory coalescing; block size changes alone are unlikely to help.
Unroll the rank-16 accumulation aggressively and keep the 16 coefficients in registers to reduce loop/control overhead.
Minimize extra global memory traffic in the fused epilogue/update path, since the remaining opportunity is bandwidth-bound output read-modify-write work.
Benchmark specialized variants per shape class or alignment class rather than one universal kernel, because a safe scalar kernel is too conservative for all tested dimensions.
If future variants do not show a clear win on the rank-16 fused path, rely on cuBLAS plus standard library ops instead of forcing custom fusion.
